name: Distinguish Real Policy From Invented Outs (and Push Past)
description: When a rep cites 'policy' to refuse a request, determine whether the policy is (a) a real written rule, (b) a system limitation, (c) a department-level guideline, or (d) an improvised refusal phrased as policy. Each one is pushed past differently.

context.when_to_use: Any refusal that includes the words 'policy', 'system won't let me', 'we don't do that', 'against the rules', 'I'm not allowed', or 'my supervisor said no'.
preconditions: Agent has the original ask clearly in mind and can rephrase it.
Agent has access to any prior written communication from the company that bears on the ask.
Agent has authority to escalate at least once before disengaging.

principles: There are four kinds of 'policy' refusals; treating them as the same is the single most common failure mode in customer-service negotiation.
Real policy has a name, a section, a document, and survives escalation. Invented outs do not survive even one careful follow-up.
Asking for the policy SOURCE is not adversarial — it's the most basic compliance question. Phrased as curiosity, it disarms.
Reps invent outs when they don't know the answer and don't want to admit it. Make admitting easier than inventing.
When you can't get past invented policy with the current rep, change the rep — don't keep arguing.
Never demand the policy in writing as a threat. Ask for it as 'help me understand'.

phrases.ask_for_name: Got it. Could you tell me what that policy is called or what section it's in? I'd love to look at the exact language.
phrases.ask_for_written: Could you send me that policy in writing — a link, an email, or a screenshot? I want to make sure I'm interpreting it the same way you are.
phrases.ask_for_authority: I appreciate that. Just to understand — is this a company-wide policy, a department policy, or your own judgement call here? All of those are valid; I just want to know which.
phrases.soft_supervisor: I don't think this is something you and I can resolve at this level. Could you bring in a supervisor or whoever owns this policy? Not a complaint — I just want to talk to the right person.
phrases.reframe_around_outcome: Let's set the policy aside for a moment. What's the OUTCOME the policy is trying to prevent? Maybe we can solve that a different way.
phrases.exception_request: I understand the general rule. Is there an exception process — a manager override, a one-time courtesy, a hardship review?

tactics.name: Classify the refusal
tactics.script: Tag mentally: (a) WRITTEN policy — rep can cite section, (b) SYSTEM limitation — rep says tool doesn't allow, (c) GUIDELINE — department or team norm, (d) INVENTED — rep is improvising. Each gets a different play.

tactics.name: Ask for the source by name
tactics.script: Use `ask_for_name`. Tone curious, not challenging. Real policies have names. Invented ones don't.

tactics.name: Distinguish company / department / personal
tactics.when: Rep can't immediately produce the policy name.
tactics.script: Use `ask_for_authority`. Offers the rep three plausible answers — and only one of them ('personal judgement') is recoverable by escalation.

tactics.name: Reframe around outcome
tactics.when: Policy is real but the user's underlying need is legitimate.
tactics.script: Use `reframe_around_outcome`. Real policies usually exist to prevent a specific harm. If you can demonstrate the harm doesn't apply, an exception path often opens.

tactics.name: Ask about exception process
tactics.script: Use `exception_request`. Even hard policies have escalation paths — manager overrides, hardship review, regulatory complaint, retention discretion.

tactics.name: Escalate one level
tactics.script: Use `soft_supervisor`. Do this ONCE. Don't keep escalating with the same call — the supervisor is the policy authority; if they say the same thing with detail, the policy is probably real.

tactics.name: Reset by callback
tactics.when: Invented out won't budge and rep refuses to escalate.
tactics.script: Hang up politely. Call back. Different rep, different judgement. See `reset-by-callback`.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep says 'our policy doesn't allow refunds after 30 days'.
consider: Written policy, accurately stated — most common.
Written policy with exception clauses the rep didn't mention.
Department guideline, but a manager has override authority.
Rep's improv; no such written limit exists.
Policy is correct as stated but doesn't apply to the user's situation (e.g., billing error vs. buyer's remorse).

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep says 'the system won't let me'.
consider: Literally true at their permission level — a supervisor has the same screen with more buttons.
True at this department; another department's tool has the option.
False; rep doesn't want to use a manual override they're allowed to use.
Workaround exists (manual ticket, escalation form) that the rep hasn't offered.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Ask: 'Who or what team has a tool that CAN do this?' Refusal to answer suggests case (c).

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep says 'my supervisor already said no'.
consider: Rep checked with a peer, not a supervisor.
Supervisor refused a misframed version of your ask.
No one was actually asked; this is a deflection.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Politely ask to speak with the supervisor directly. Reframe the ask in your own words once you have them.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep says 'that's just how it works'.
consider: Genuine policy they can't cite by name.
Industry norm being passed off as company policy.
Rep doesn't know and is hoping you'll drop it.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Ask `ask_for_authority`. Almost always reveals it as guideline or judgement, both of which are negotiable.

boundaries: Do NOT call the rep a liar even when the policy is clearly invented. Curiosity > confrontation.
Do NOT keep arguing past the second 'no' from a supervisor — change reps instead.
Do NOT demand 'put it in writing' as a threat — request it as clarification.
Do NOT claim a policy you read somewhere unless you can cite it accurately.
success_signals: Rep changes phrasing from 'policy' to 'guideline' or 'usually'.
Rep mentions an exception path or escalation route unprompted.
Supervisor adjusts the answer the front-line rep gave.
Rep produces the policy in writing and the language doesn't actually forbid the ask.
Rep volunteers a workaround that achieves the same outcome.
failure_signals: Supervisor confirms the front-line rep's refusal with detail, citation, and consistency.
Multiple reps over multiple calls give the same refusal in the same language.
Policy is in writing and unambiguous.
Rep escalates to ending the call instead of escalating to a supervisor.

exit_strategy.on_success: Document the exception or workaround granted, with the rep's name, time, and a confirmation number. Get it in writing.
exit_strategy.on_failure: End politely. Note the exact wording of the refusal. Decide whether to (a) callback for a different rep, (b) submit a written complaint citing the policy text, or (c) accept the outcome.
follow_ups: If invented-policy is suspected, attempt one callback. If the second rep gives a different answer, document both for written escalation.
For regulated industries (banking, insurance, healthcare, telecom), invented outs can be escalated to a regulator — note for the user.

required_user_info: What the user actually wants and what they'd settle for.
Any prior written communication relevant to the policy.
How much time the user is willing to spend on escalation.
